title: Diving Deep Into ‘Avatar: The Way of Water’ and ‘Triangle of Sadness’
format: Sean, Amanda, and Van Lathan open on the Oscar race and who got shut out of it, then take a second pass at two Best Picture nominees set on the water: Avatar: The Way of Water and Triangle of Sadness.

segments.heading: The Oscar race and who got left out

films.title: The Woman King
films.note: Van and Amanda's case study in how a commercial hit with a bona fide star and a real cultural conversation still missed every major nomination, a Sony release with a distributor that hasn't fared well with the Academy lately.

films.title: Women Talking
films.note: Amanda likes it, but notes it landed a Best Picture slot with almost no box office or cultural footprint. It's the movie they keep pitting against The Woman King.

films.title: Everything Everywhere All at Once
films.note: Got the loudest applause at the Academy luncheon; Sean has long pegged it the presumptive winner.

films.title: Top Gun: Maverick
films.note: Van's best-of-the-year pick; the Spielberg-thanks-Cruise-for-saving-theaters moment at the luncheon left Sean wondering whether it could actually win.

films.title: TÁR
films.note: Van has seen it two and a half times; a running bit about whether Amanda has too much Lydia Tár energy.

films.title: Down with the King
films.note: Van's example of a great performance, Freddie Gibbs, that won't generate any Oscar buzz.

films.title: Avatar: The Way of Water
films.note: $2.2 billion and change; nominated for Picture, Sound, Production Design, and Visual Effects. Van was floored: a simple family-in-peril story anchoring generational effects. Sean admires the engineering but keeps snagging on the storytelling seams; Amanda found the mythology overwhelming.

films.title: Avatar
films.note: The one Van dug as a world without needing a sequel; Sean's touchstone for a Cameron movie that dazzled in theaters and shrank on a 40-inch TV.

films.title: Titanic
films.note: Van loves it and defends Cal as pure one-note evil; Sean plays the skeptic and gets branded a Titanologist for it.

films.title: Terminator 2: Judgment Day
films.note: Cited as Cameron's binary-villain craft: the T-1000 with almost no lines, a brainless killing machine that makes the hero human.

films.title: Aliens
films.note: Part of the early-Cameron run that took Sean's breath away when he was young.

films.title: The Abyss
films.note: Loved, with flaws Sean admits to.

films.title: True Lies
films.note: Named as the kind of movie Cameron is done making now that he's blue forever.

films.title: Piranha II: The Spawning
films.note: Even the Cameron film he disowned has greatness in it, Van argues.

films.title: Murina
films.note: Antoneta Alamat Kusijanović's seaside debut, exec-produced by Scorsese; Cameron lent it his name because it's a simple story set on the water.

films.title: The Matrix
films.note: Sean's example of lore bloat: a sleek, minimalist first film that later drowns under its own council and mythology, the trap he half-sees in Avatar.

films.title: The Patriot
films.note: Van's gym story about Jason Isaacs, who insisted his simple-villain writing did the work: kill a kid early, coast on evil the rest of the way.

films.title: Triangle of Sadness
films.note: The Palme d'Or winner, nominated for Picture, Director, and Original Screenplay. Van is all the way in on the class-warfare satire; Sean finds the overwrittenness the point on a rewatch but still can't get past the middle act; Amanda credits the Dolly de Leon reveal and the ambiguous ending.

films.title: Force Majeure
films.note: Östlund's treatise on frail modern masculinity.

films.title: The Square
films.note: His art-world satire; the other Östlund movie Van has seen.

films.title: Play
films.note: The early Swedish film about a social experiment among kids, smaller than what he makes now.

films.title: The Menu
films.note: Amanda's comparison point: a more on-the-label eat-the-rich satire she found less engaging than this.

films.title: Glass Onion: A Knives Out Mystery
films.note: Named alongside The Menu and Succession as the current glut of rich-people-are-frauds storytelling the hosts try to explain.

films.title: Babylon
films.note: Sean's passionate mess to defend; Van gets the asshole-of-LA sequence and the closing movie-magic montage but keeps needling Sean about what Babylon is actually trying to say.

films.title: Singin' in the Rain
films.note: Anchors the Babylon finale montage that made Van start screaming.

films.title: La La Land
films.note: Invoked for the silhouette what-could-have-been ending, Chazelle's recurring move.

films.title: First Man
films.note: Another Chazelle closer: the gaze between two people living inside a foreign land.

films.title: Whiplash
films.note: Andrew Neiman staring at Buddy Rich; part of the Chazelle ending run.

Glass Onion: A Knives Out Mystery.title: Glass Onion: A Knives Out Mystery
